skill.name: service-mesh-expert
skill.description: Expert service mesh architect specializing in Istio, Linkerd, and cloud-native networking patterns. Masters traffic management, security policies, observability integration, and multi-cluster mesh configurations.
skill.summary: Service mesh architecture expert for Istio and Linkerd deployments with focus on zero-trust security and traffic management.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 4 patterns that are all false positives. Line 22 uses Markdown backticks for documentation reference, not shell execution. Lines 3, 46, and 60 contain no cryptographic code - they reference mTLS conceptually in documentation. This is a markdown-only skill with no executable code, external commands, or security risks.

content.user_title: Design Service Mesh Architectures with Istio and Linkerd
content.value_statement: Microservices need secure, observable communication without complexity. This skill provides expert guidance on Istio and Linkerd deployments with zero-trust networking and traffic management.
actual_capabilities: Design and configure Istio or Linkerd service mesh topologies for Kubernetes clusters
Implement mTLS for zero-trust service-to-service communication
Configure traffic management policies including routing, load balancing, and circuit breakers
Set up distributed tracing and observability with mesh telemetry
Plan multi-cluster mesh federation and cross-cloud deployments
Create canary and blue-green deployment strategies with traffic splitting
limitations: Cannot directly execute kubectl or istioctl commands - provides configuration only
Requires manual implementation of generated configurations in your cluster
Does not replace need for production testing and validation
Mesh configurations may need adjustment for specific cloud provider environments

use_cases.title: Kubernetes Platform Engineer
use_cases.description: Deploy Istio service mesh with mTLS enforcement and traffic policies for a production microservices platform handling high-availability requirements.

use_cases.title: DevOps Team Lead
use_cases.description: Implement canary deployments with traffic splitting and automated rollback using Istio VirtualService and DestinationRule configurations.

use_cases.title: Security Architect
use_cases.description: Design zero-trust network architecture with service-to-service authentication using mTLS and AuthorizationPolicy enforcement across all namespaces.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Service Mesh Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up Istio service mesh on my Kubernetes cluster. I have 3 namespaces (dev, staging, prod) and need basic mTLS between services. What are the installation steps and initial configuration?

prompt_templates.title: Traffic Routing Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to route 90% of traffic to version-1 and 10% to version-2 of my payment service. Create the Istio VirtualService and DestinationRule YAML configurations with explanation.

prompt_templates.title: Circuit Breaker Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a circuit breaker configuration for my order service that handles upstream failures gracefully. Include connection pool settings, outlier detection, and retry policies with Istio.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Cluster Federation
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a multi-cluster Istio mesh across AWS EKS and GCP GKE. Include requirements for cross-cluster service discovery, certificate management, and traffic federation between the two meshes.

output_examples.output: Step-by-step PeerAuthentication and DestinationRule configurations to enforce strict mTLS cluster-wide, starting with permissive mode migration path and verification commands to confirm encryption.
Systematic troubleshooting checklist including sidecar injection verification, VirtualService routing analysis, authorization policy conflicts, and istioctl debug commands with expected outputs.

best_practices: Start with PERMISSIVE mTLS mode and gradually migrate to STRICT after verifying all services communicate correctly
Implement circuit breakers and retry policies before production deployment, not after failures occur
Use namespace-level policy isolation to apply different security and traffic rules per environment
anti_patterns: Enabling strict mTLS cluster-wide without testing in permissive mode first - causes immediate service disruptions
Skipping circuit breaker configuration assuming services are reliable - cascading failures will occur under load
Over-provisioning sidecar resources without monitoring actual CPU and memory usage - increases costs unnecessarily

faq.question: What is the difference between Istio and Linkerd for my use case?
faq.answer: Istio offers comprehensive traffic management, security, and observability with more configuration options but higher complexity. Linkerd provides simpler mTLS and basic observability with lower resource overhead. Choose Istio for complex routing needs, Linkerd for straightforward mTLS with minimal operational burden.

faq.question: Does service mesh add significant latency to my services?
faq.answer: Typical sidecar proxy overhead is 3-10ms per request for mTLS and routing. Linkerd generally has lower overhead (2-5ms) than Istio (5-10ms). The security and observability benefits usually outweigh the latency cost, but measure your specific workload before production deployment.

faq.question: Can I use service mesh with non-Kubernetes workloads?
faq.answer: Istio supports VMs through istio-vm integration, allowing hybrid deployments. Linkerd requires Kubernetes. For mixed environments, Istio is the better choice with proper VM sidecar proxy configuration.

faq.question: How do I handle database connections through the service mesh?
faq.answer: Database traffic typically bypasses the mesh using traffic exclusion rules. Configure sidecar interception exclusions for database ports, or use egress gateways for controlled external access with proper TLS origination.

faq.question: What monitoring should I set up for the service mesh?
faq.answer: Monitor sidecar proxy CPU and memory, request latency percentencies (p50, p95, p99), error rates, mTLS connection status, and configuration sync health. Integrate with Prometheus and Grafana using built-in Istio or Linkerd dashboards.

faq.question: How do I roll back a problematic mesh configuration?
faq.answer: Keep versioned Istio configurations in Git. Use kubectl apply with previous manifest versions for immediate rollback. For critical issues, disable sidecar injection at namespace level and redeploy pods to bypass the mesh temporarily.
